steps.title: AIG: 4 ANDs, two POs (f = a+b, g = ¬(a+b)·d)
steps.narrative:
Source: resub_win.blif. The user wrote f = ((a+b)·((a+b)+c)) the literal way: n5 = ¬a·¬b, n6 = n5·¬c, f = ¬n5·¬n6. The double-cover collapses to f = a+b, but neither rewrite (cone too small for a 4-input library hit) nor refactor (no algebraic simplification) catches it.

resub spots it via simulation: f's 32-pattern bit-vector matches ¬n5 exactly. The fix is just a wire redirect.

panes.title: Per-node pipeline
panes.body:
1. reconvergence cut + MFFC at the candidate
  2. collect divisors (cone-internal nodes outside MFFC + leaves)
  3. simulate everything with 32-pattern random vectors
  4. k-step ladder Divs0…Divs3: try expressing the candidate's sim as 0…3 ANDs of divisors
  5. SAT-verify the first hit, commit

steps.title: Reconvergence cut + MFFC at f
steps.narrative:
Abc_NodeFindCut(pManCut, f, 0) with default nCutMax = 8 returns leaves {a, b, c}. The MFFC is computed via the boundary trick: only nodes whose every fanout is inside the cone become dereferenced.

n5 has fanout 3 — to n6, to f, and to g. The g consumer is outside f's cone, so n5 stays alive. Only {f, n6} joins the MFFC. (The trace's mffc=2 matches.)

steps.title: Collect divisors — leaves + non-MFFC internal
steps.narrative:
Abc_ManResubCollectDivs gathers everything in the cone that isn't in the MFFC: the cut leaves themselves plus internal nodes with external fanouts. Each becomes a candidate building-block.

For our cut at f: divisors = { a, b, c, n5 } — three PIs and one already-existing AND. n5 is the only non-leaf divisor; it's exactly what will save us.

steps.title: Simulate every divisor and the candidate
steps.narrative:
Each node gets a 32-pattern bit-vector. PIs are seeded with the standard ABC patterns; internal nodes are computed by AND-ing fanin sims with edge polarity applied. f's stored value ¬n5·¬n6 matches 0xEEEEEEEE across all 32 patterns.

The Divs0 step (next) just compares these vectors.

steps.title: Divs0 — sim(f) == ¬sim(n5) ✓
steps.narrative:
Abc_ManResubDivs0 walks the divisor pool and tests sim(f) == sim(d) or sim(f) == ¬sim(d) for each divisor d. The match: 0xEEEEEEEE == ¬0x11111111. So f ≡ ¬n5 on all 32 sampled patterns.

Sim-equivalence isn't proof — could be aliasing — so a SAT call (Abc_NtkResubVerify) confirms the equivalence under the full Boolean function. CNF here is tiny; SAT closes in 0 conflicts. Replacement: just n5 with the output-complement bit set.

panes.body: If Divs0 had failed, the ladder would climb: Divs1 tries one new AND of divisor pairs, Divs12 tries one + two, Divs2 tries two new ANDs, Divs3 three. Default -N 1 stops at Divs1; resyn3 uses higher rungs.

steps.title: Commit — redirect PO_f → ¬n5; n6, f dangle
steps.narrative:
The replacement is converted to a 0-AND Dec_Graph_t and committed via Dec_GraphUpdateNetwork. PO_f's edge now points to n5 with the inverter set. n6 loses its only fanout (was f), f loses its only fanout (was PO_f) — both go dangling and are reclaimed by reference counting.

n5 stays because g still uses it. The resulting AIG has 2 ANDs (n5, g), level 2.

panes.body: Resub finds gain that rewrite + refactor cannot: it can collapse a sub-cone into an existing internal signal whenever the Boolean relationship coincidentally holds. It's sensitive to the divisor pool — that's why resyn3 runs three different cut sizes consecutively.
